Bidhan Chandra Roy

# Bidhan Chandra Roy

### Doctors Day

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Jul 01, 2021

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

Share

Doctors Day is celebrated in honor of Dr.Bidhan Chandra Roy, 2nd CM of West Bengal, one of Bengal's longest serving CMs, s a freedom fighter, physician. As CM he founded the cities of Durgapur, Kalyani, Salt Lake City(now Bidhannagar), Ashok Nagar and Habra.

He was born on July 1st, 1882 and passed away on the same date in 1962, symbolically bookending a life of profound service.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!1-vM!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F235cf0af-fb8d-4f57-b90f-742d731b8aa7_200x265.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!1-vM!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F235cf0af-fb8d-4f57-b90f-742d731b8aa7_200x265.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

He was born at Bankipore in 1882, a neighborhood of Patna, and did his Maths Honors from Patna University. A Brilliant student, he , got admission at both Bengal Engg College and Kolkata Medical College and opted for the latter.

"Whatever thy hands findeth to do, do it with thy might.”

This quote at Kolkata Medical College was to be B.C.Roy's motivation always. With his father retiring as Dy.Collector, he had to support himself on a scholarship while studying medicine, and lived frugally.

Though the Bengal Partition stoked a nationalist fervor, however he felt that he needed to complete his medicine first and joinedthe Provincial Health Service, he , impressed with his dedication and hard work, and also had a private practice.

He also applied for the St. Bartholomew's Hospital in London, he was however rejected first, due to his Asian background.He did not give up, kept applying, and after 30 trials, he finally got admission at St.Bartholomew's in London.

Completing his MRCP and FRCS from England, Bidhan Roy returned to India in 1911, and he began to teach at Kolkata Medical College.He believed that only a healthy, fit population could fight for Swaraj well, and dedicated his efforts towards that

Dr. B.C. Roy wasn’t just a physician or politician—he was an architect of social transformation.

Jadavpur TB Hospital, Chittaranjan Seva Sadan, Chittaranjan Cancer Hospital were set up by him. At a time when tuberculosis was a public health crisis, establishing a dedicated center showed his medical acumen. Naming two institutions after Chittaranjan Das, a nationalist mentor and symbol of sacrifice, also reflects Roy’s layered sense of homage and purpose.

The Chittaranjan Seva Sadan s wasn’t merely a hospital—it was a revolution in women’s health and empowerment. Training women in nursing at a time when it wasn’t widely accepted challenged deep-rooted social norms and opened up new roles for Indian women in public service.

When Rangoon fell and the Japanese threat loomed over Kolkata, Roy’s response wasn’t rhetorical—it was tactical and humane. Air raid shelters in educational institutions speak of a leader who anticipated danger and took preventive action to protect civilians, especially the young.

Acting as Gandhiji’s personal physician and confidant, he bridged the worlds of science and satyagraha. It wasn't just that he could treat the Mahatma’s ailments—it’s that he understood his ideals. That proximity likely shaped his own philosophy of Swaraj rooted in public welfare, not just political autonomy.

In 1925, Bidhan Roy, won from Barrackpore as an Independent, defeating Surendranath Banerjee, a nationalist stalwart, that wasn't merely a political win—it marked a generational shift.

Roy brought a new kind of leadership, one shaped not just by oratory, but by service.His role in organizing the 1929 civil disobedience movement in Bengal showed that beneath the quiet demeanour of a doctor was a deeply committed revolutionary.

As Mayor of Kolkata, he worked for providing better education, better health care, improved lighting, water supply.This was grassroots governance at its finest. He treated the city not as an abstract machine, but as a living organism.

When he took over as CM of Bengal in 1948, he was faced with the immense challenge of rehabilitating Partition refugees, apart from the communal riots and widespread unemployment.Refugee rehabilitation wasn’t simply about housing—it was about restoring identity and hope. And yet, he didn’t crumble. He built.

Within 3 years, he managed to ensure law and order returned back to Bengal, and the Partition refugees were resettled. To stabilize post-Partition Bengal in such a short time speaks to his clarity of purpose and administrative genius.

The Durgapur Steel Plant was not merely an industrial milestone—it became a symbol of self-reliance. Forging steel while forging identity. While Salt Lake City, was born from his vision of urban dignity, later named Bidhannagar in reverence—this township is a living monument to his planning foresight.

In 1961 he was awarded the Bharat Ratna, richly deserved, and he passed away in 1962 at the age of 80, on his birthday. His contribution to Bengal would forever be remembered though.
